results.possibleIdentity: Max Vernon Mathews (1926–2011), commonly referred to as M.V. Mathews, was an American electrical engineer and a pioneering figure in the field of computer music. He joined Bell Telephone Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey, in 1955 and became the Director of the Acoustical and Behavioral Research Center from 1962 to 1985. Mathews is widely recognized as the "father of computer music" for developing the first computer program capable of synthesizing digital audio, known as MUSIC I, in 1957. He continued to evolve this software through multiple versions, culminating in MUSIC V by 1969. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was deeply involved in the intersection of technology and the arts, collaborating with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He was one of the notable engineers from Bell Labs who participated in the landmark "9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering" performances in 1966 and contributed to various art-technology collaborations throughout his career. His later work included the development of the GROOVE real-time performance system (1970) and the Radio Baton conductor's wand. He was a member of the National Academy of Sciences and the National Academy of Engineering.

He continued to evolve this software through multiple versions, culminating in MUSIC V by 1969.
In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was deeply involved in the intersection of technology and the arts, collaborating with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).
